subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: compaction: support non-movable compaction for pageblock requests

2026-06-26.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's compaction code to support non-movable compaction for pageblock requests. It relaxes the restrictions on type mixing and pollution, allowing whole-block requests to scan blocks of other types. The exception is CMA (Contiguous Memory Allocation) blocks, which remain strictly typed due to their sticky nature. The patch aims to improve the efficiency of defrag_mode by enabling non-movable direct compaction, making it more useful for producing whole blocks that can be converted wholesale on allocation.

reviews.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
reviews.summary: Author questioned the motivation behind restricting async compaction to pageblocks of same migratetype, suggesting that this behavior may not be desired and could be improved by applying the block pollution guard only when allocation fallbacks are likely.
reviews.raw_body:
Let's see. Originally it came from here:

commit 9927af740b1b9b1e769310bd0b91425e8047b803
Author: Mel Gorman <mel@csn.ul.ie>
Date:   Thu Jan 13 15:45:59 2011 -0800

    mm: compaction: perform a faster migration scan when migrating asynchronously

This limited async scanners to movable blocks. By keeping them to the
most productive space, it keeps their latencies down.

But then there was a follow up here:

commit 282722b0d258ec23fc79d80165418fee83f01736
Author: Vlastimil Babka <vbabka@kernel.org>
Date:   Mon May 8 15:54:49 2017 -0700

    mm, compaction: restrict async compaction to pageblocks of same migratetype

This made the migratetype filtering about preventing block
pollution. The patch quotes reduced extfrag numbers.

So now we have a block pollution guard that we apply only if... the
scanner is latency sensitive? :) Is this actually desired behavior?

Another way of looking at it would be this:

	/*
	 * Allocation fallbacks can spread migratable pages
	 * into non-movable blocks. This is high-effort,
	 * low-result work. Restrict it to sync scans.
	 */

reviews.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch does not prevent non-movable pages from being moved into movable blocks outside of defrag mode, and requested consideration for this scenario.
reviews.raw_body:
But also vice versa, non-movable pages into movable blocks? (without
defrag_mode?).

reviews.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing Vlastimil's concern about non-movable compaction requests being allowed to scan movable blocks, even though they are not compactable anymore. The author acknowledges this issue and explains that the behavior for movable and non-movable requests is asymmetrical, with movable requests first trying high-effort mode on unmovable blocks before escalating, whereas non-movable requests start in high-effort mode but can escalate when it doesn't produce results.
reviews.raw_body:
Uhm but those aren't compactable anymore then, right?

There is a flipside, but it isn't quite symmetrical. Movable requests
are allowed to look for movables in unmovable blocks once they become
sync (high-effort, low-result). Non-movable requests are finally
allowed to empty movable blocks once they become sync; they *start*
with the high-effort, low-result mode to avoid block contamination but
are allowed to escalate when that doesn't produce results.

I'll try to work that second part into the comment as well.

reviews.author: Zi Yan
reviews.summary: Reviewer Zi Yan suggested clarifying the definition of 'small' in the patch description, specifically that it refers to pageblock_order requests
reviews.raw_body:
"small" here means smaller than pageblock_order requests? If yes, it is
better to spell it out to prevent confusion.

2026-07-01.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's compaction code to support non-movable direct compaction for pageblock requests. It allows whole-block requests, which are essentially type neutral, to scan blocks of other types and fixes the protection gates to take the requested order into account. The only exception is CMA (Contiguous Memory Allocation) blocks, which remain strictly typed due to their sticky nature. This change aims to improve the efficiency of non-movable direct compaction, particularly in defrag_mode where whole-block production is a goal.
